results.possibleIdentity: Dr. Dennis H. Irvine was a Jamaican scientist, educator, and academic administrator who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He served as the Vice-Chancellor of the University of Guyana from 1969 to 1982 and was a prominent figure in the Caribbean scientific and cultural landscape. Originally a Professor of Chemistry who studied at the University of Leeds and Cambridge, his career was defined by efforts to integrate science and technology into Caribbean education and society. He was associated with the development of the Caribbean Examinations Council (CXC) and later served as the Director of Caribbean Programmes for the Commonwealth of Learning and a UNESCO consultant. His association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) likely stems from his high-level involvement in regional science and technology policy and his patronage of the arts; the University of Guyana established the 'Dennis Irvine Award' for students who make the greatest contribution to the university's cultural life. Historical records from LACMA's 'All Impossible Deeds' exhibit link the name Irvine to E.A.T. projects in Jamaica during this period, where E.A.T. sought to foster collaborations between technological development and artistic expression in local communities.

results.possibleIdentity: David Rothenberg is a mathematician, computer scientist, composer, and theorist who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is recognized as a pioneer in the application of mathematical models and pattern recognition to music theory and perception. His historical significance is notably linked to his work on 'adaptive pattern recognition' and the development of formal languages for both audio and visual perception. In 1969, he published a seminal technical report titled 'A Pattern-Recognition Model Applied to the Perception of Pitch' for the Air Force Office of Scientific Research (AFOSR), followed by a 1970 report on an 'Adaptive Pattern Representation and Recognition System.' He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, specifically through the Los Angeles branch (E.A.T.–LA), where he collaborated with and was cited alongside computer art pioneers like John Whitney (Sr.). His research often sought to create inter-meshed audio-visual languages and explored the boundaries between art, science, and communication, including government-contracted work on communication formats for potentially exchanging ideas with non-human or alien species. His theoretical work, such as his 'Mathematical Model for the Perception of Tones,' is archived in collections of major 20th-century composers like Otto Luening.

Diane Rothenberg is an anthropologist and author who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s, particularly within the 'ethnopoetics' movement. Often working alongside her partner, the poet Jerome Rothenberg, she conducted extensive field research on the Allegany Seneca Reservation in New York during this period. Her work focused on the intersections of oral tradition, female identity (as seen in her book 'The Mothers of the Nation'), and the adaptation of indigenous art and culture into modern poetic and performance contexts. While primarily known as a scholar and anthropologist, her contributions to the emerging discourse on cross-cultural poetics and her collaborative editing of influential anthologies like 'Symposium of the Whole' place her within the historical artistic and intellectual circles of the 1970s.

results.possibleIdentity: Aaron Marcus (born May 22, 1943) is an American artist, graphic designer, and pioneer in computer graphics and human-computer interaction who was highly active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is recognized as the first professional graphic designer to work full-time with computer graphics, beginning as a research intern at AT&T Bell Laboratories in 1967. During his time at Bell Labs, he was part of the influential community of artists and engineers that intersected with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization founded by Billy Klüver. Marcus's early work included creating ASCII art and programming some of the first virtual reality art/design spaces, such as 'Cybernetic Landscape I' (1971–1973), which he developed while a faculty member at Princeton University (1968–1977). His artistic output from the 1970s includes computer-assisted works like 'Noise Barrier' (1974) and 'Radioactive Jukebox' (1972). His art has been exhibited at major venues, including the Victoria and Albert Museum and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, and he is widely cited as a seminal figure in the transition of graphic design into the digital age.
